## (a) Implementation

```dafny
function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id
{
  if x.width >= PAD then x
  else Id(x.kind, x.value, PAD)
}
```

## (b) Lemma specification

```dafny
lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures
    // kind and numeric value are never changed
    Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind &&
    Canonicalize(x).value == x.value &&
    // result is at canonical width: at least PAD, and at least PAD is the minimum,
    // but ids already at >= PAD digits are returned unchanged
    Canonicalize(x).width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD) &&
    // result is wellformed (its width can hold its value)
    Wellformed(Canonicalize(x)) &&
    // result is at minimum-4 canonical width
    Canonicalize(x).width >= PAD &&
    // canonicalization is idempotent (a no-op on already-canonical ids)
    Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)
{ }
```

### Why this is precise and complete

- **Kind never changes**: `Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind`.
- **Value never changes** (same entity): `Canonicalize(x).value == x.value`.
- **Width is exactly canonical**: the conditional `if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD` pins the width down completely — already-wide ids are unchanged, narrow ids become exactly `PAD`. This is the key clause; it forbids both under-padding and over-padding.
- **Result wellformed / `width >= PAD`**: these are derivable from the width clause but stated explicitly to make the canonical-width guarantee self-contained.
- **Idempotence**: `Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)` captures "canonicalizing an already-canonical id is a no-op."

Note that since `Wellformed(x)` gives `x.width >= NumDigits(x.value)`, the result with width `PAD` is still wellformed: when `x.width < PAD`, `NumDigits(x.value) <= x.width < PAD`, so `PAD >= NumDigits(x.value)`.
